content.text: AWS operates one of the largest private global networks in the world — a dedicated fiber optic infrastructure connecting all AWS regions, Availability Zones, and edge locations. Traffic between AWS services and between your application and AWS edge locations travels on this private network rather than the public internet, yielding lower latency, higher throughput, and more consistent performance.

attrs.alt: AWS global network backbone: private fiber connecting regions globally vs dashed public internet path; Direct Connect customer attachment point

content.text: The distinction between the AWS backbone and the public internet is fundamental to understanding latency and reliability in AWS architectures:
BGP routing through dozens of third-party ASes
Consistent — no routing flaps or congestion from unrelated traffic
Variable — shared with all internet traffic globally
Variable — depends on ISP and transit congestion
Terabit-scale fiber with AWS traffic only
Not exposed to public internet scanning/MITM

When you transfer data between two AWS services in the same region — say, from EC2 to S3 — that traffic never touches the public internet. It travels on AWS's internal network at data-center speeds. This is true even if both services have public IP addresses.
The backbone consists of three layers:

— AWS has invested in and co-owns multiple undersea fiber cables connecting continents: Transpacific (US to Japan, Singapore, Australia), Transatlantic (US to Europe), and cables along other routes. These provide terabit capacity with redundant fiber pairs.

— within continents, AWS operates land-based fiber connecting regions to each other and to AWS Direct Connect locations. Traffic from us-east-1 to us-west-2 travels on AWS-owned fiber across North America.
— within and between AZs in a region, AWS operates a high-bandwidth low-latency network fabric. AZs are connected by multiple redundant links at speeds measured in terabits per second.

AWS does not publish specifics about cable routes or capacity, but disclosed investments include participation in the MAREA cable (Spain-US), the SJC2 cable (US-Japan), and others. The scale allows AWS to route around failures by shifting traffic to alternate paths within minutes.
Your application traffic enters the AWS private network at the closest AWS edge location (Point of Presence). From there it travels privately to the destination region:
User's request reaches the nearest AWS edge location via public internet.
Edge location terminates the TCP/TLS connection and forwards the request over the private backbone to the origin region.
The private backbone provides the high-bandwidth, low-latency path for the bulk of data transfer.
The origin service in the region responds; the reply traverses the backbone to the edge and then the public internet for the last mile to the user.

This is the pattern used by CloudFront, AWS Global Accelerator, and API Gateway edge-optimized endpoints. The public internet is only used for the first and last mile — the network segment you have no control over — while the longer, more critical path uses AWS's controlled backbone.

AWS Global Accelerator is the service that explicitly exposes the backbone for non-HTTP workloads and TCP/UDP acceleration. It provides two static anycast IP addresses that map to AWS edge locations worldwide. User packets are routed to the nearest edge PoP and then forwarded over the backbone:
— two static IP addresses announced from all Global Accelerator edge locations simultaneously. DNS returns these IPs; the user's OS routes packets to the nearest location via anycast.
— TCP and UDP (unlike CloudFront which is HTTP/S). Use for gaming, IoT, VoIP, and real-time data streaming where raw TCP/UDP performance matters.
— targets in each region (ALB, NLB, EC2, or Elastic IP). Traffic weight is configurable per endpoint group.
— if an endpoint becomes unhealthy, Global Accelerator routes around it to the next healthy region — within the backbone, without DNS TTL delays.

CloudFront uses the backbone for origin fetches and for routing cache misses. When a CloudFront edge PoP does not have an object cached, it does not fetch it over the public internet — it fetches via the private backbone to the Regional Edge Cache (REC) or directly to the origin:
Edge PoP → Regional Edge Cache → Origin: all on the AWS backbone.
If the origin is an S3 bucket or an EC2-hosted server in the same AWS region, the entire end-to-end path after the user's last-mile request is on AWS infrastructure.
Transfer Acceleration for S3 also uses this pattern: uploads go to the nearest PoP, then over the backbone to S3.

Custom origins outside AWS (your on-premises server) are still on the public internet for the origin leg — only the edge-to-origin segment benefits from the backbone when the origin is an AWS service.
Direct Connect allows enterprise customers to connect their on-premises networks directly into the AWS backbone, bypassing the public internet entirely for all traffic:
— 1 Gbps, 10 Gbps, or 100 Gbps port at an AWS Direct Connect location (co-location facility where AWS terminates backbone fiber). You cross-connect your router to the AWS port.
— smaller bandwidth (50 Mbps – 10 Gbps) provided by a Direct Connect partner. Easier to provision, no co-location required.
— connects to your VPC via a virtual interface. Traffic to EC2, RDS, S3 (via VPC endpoint) stays on the backbone.
— connects to AWS public endpoints (S3 public endpoint, DynamoDB, other global services). Traffic uses the backbone instead of internet.
— connects to AWS Transit Gateway, enabling connectivity to multiple VPCs across accounts and regions from a single Direct Connect connection.

# Direct Connect is provisioned via the console or AWS partner;
# Terraform manages the virtual interface after the physical port is ready.

# Private VIF — connects to VGW in your VPC
resource "aws_dx_private_virtual_interface" "corp" {
  connection_id    = "dxcon-xxxx"  # From console after physical setup
  name             = "corp-to-vpc"
  vlan             = 101
  address_family   = "ipv4"
  bgp_asn          = 65000  # Your on-prem ASN
  amazon_address   = "169.254.254.1/30"
  customer_address = "169.254.254.2/30"
  bgp_auth_key     = var.bgp_md5_key
  vpn_gateway_id   = aws_vpn_gateway.main.id
}

# Direct Connect Gateway for multi-region connectivity
resource "aws_dx_gateway" "main" {
  name            = "corp-dxgw"
  amazon_side_asn = "64512"
}
The AWS backbone is designed with multiple levels of redundancy. A single fiber cut — even a major submarine cable break — does not cause regional outages because:
Multiple redundant fiber paths exist between regions. AWS routes traffic around the failure automatically.
Submarine cables use multiple fiber pairs. A single pair break degrades capacity but does not take down connectivity.
Terrestrial routes supplement submarine cables. If transatlantic submarine cables are impacted, terrestrial trans-Polar or trans-Arctic routes provide backup.
BGP re-convergence on the backbone is under AWS's control, meaning it happens in seconds — not the minutes or hours that public internet BGP re-convergence can take.

AWS does not publish its backbone topology or SLAs for inter-region connectivity. However, the backbone's resilience is a component of the overall availability guarantees for services like S3 Cross-Region Replication and DynamoDB Global Tables.
All inter-region traffic between AWS services travels on the backbone. This has practical implications for architecture:
EC2 ↔ EC2, different AZ, same region
S3 VPC endpoint keeps traffic internal
VPC Traffic Routing and the Backbone
Within a region, VPC traffic to AWS services can be kept on the backbone (and off the internet) using VPC endpoints:
— for S3 and DynamoDB. Free. Add a route in your route table; traffic to those services stays on the backbone without leaving your VPC.
— for almost all other AWS services (KMS, SSM, ECR, CloudWatch, etc.). Creates an ENI in your subnet; traffic stays on the backbone and in your VPC.
— interface endpoints have VPC security groups applied, giving you fine-grained control over which resources can call which services.

# S3 gateway endpoint — free, keeps S3 traffic on backbone
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "s3" {
  vpc_id       = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name = "com.amazonaws.us-east-1.s3"
  route_table_ids = aws_route_table.private[*].id
}

# KMS interface endpoint — for encryption calls from private subnets
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "kms" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.us-east-1.kms"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = aws_subnet.private[*].id
  security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.endpoints.id]
  private_dns_enabled = true
}
You can measure the latency benefit of the backbone versus the public internet using AWS tools:
AWS Global Accelerator speed comparison tool
— available in the console. Measures latency from your browser to a test endpoint through both Global Accelerator (backbone) and the public internet.
— monitors availability and performance of internet traffic to your application from different geographies. Shows you where the public internet is degraded.
— verifies network paths within a VPC, confirming traffic is not unexpectedly exiting to the internet.
When Does the Backbone Not Apply?
The backbone covers AWS-internal traffic. Some traffic still uses the public internet:
Traffic from EC2 instances to the public internet (egress) uses the backbone to the nearest edge, then transitions to the public internet.
Traffic to non-AWS servers (your on-premises data center) travels over the internet unless you use Direct Connect or Site-to-Site VPN.
Traffic to third-party SaaS APIs (Stripe, Twilio, etc.) uses the public internet regardless of where your EC2 instance is.
S3 Transfer Acceleration uses the backbone for the upload path (user → PoP → backbone → S3), but not for access over the standard S3 endpoint from the public internet.

AWS Transit Gateway (TGW) acts as a regional network hub, and its inter-region peering attachment lets you extend that hub across regions entirely over the AWS backbone. Traffic between two peered TGWs never leaves the private fiber.
— a dedicated logical link between two TGWs in different regions; each side accepts/rejects the peering request.
— inter-region peering uses static routes; dynamic BGP propagation does not cross the peering boundary.
— no hard upper limit per attachment; aggregate throughput is constrained by TGW's 50 Gbps per AZ.
— data processing at TGW ($0.02/GB) plus inter-region transfer (backbone rates) apply.
Empirical measurements comparing traffic routed over the AWS backbone versus the public internet show consistent advantages for private routing, especially for long-haul paths.

Jitter (variance in latency) matters more than raw RTT for real-time workloads. A game server that sees 10 ms of jitter is harder to compensate for than one with a stable 80 ms RTT. The backbone's predictable queue depths and absence of arbitrary ISP peering hops produce dramatically lower jitter figures.

The backbone is private, but private does not mean unencrypted. Defense-in-depth requires encrypting traffic even on AWS-controlled fiber to protect against insider threats and hardware-level interception.
— IEEE 802.1AE Layer 2 encryption between your on-premises router and the AWS DX endpoint. Zero-latency overhead compared to IPsec tunnels.
— AWS services communicate internally over TLS. Application-layer TLS (e.g., HTTPS between microservices) adds a second encryption layer for sensitive data.
— add an IPsec tunnel over a Direct Connect public VIF for FIPS-validated encryption when regulatory requirements demand it.
— stateful packet inspection for inter-VPC and north-south traffic in hub-and-spoke TGW architectures; rules inspect traffic before it egresses to another region.
— capture metadata (src/dst IP, port, protocol, bytes) for all backbone-routed traffic entering or leaving a VPC interface; publish to CloudWatch Logs or S3 for analysis.
— traffic from private subnets through a NAT GW exits to the public internet. Move cross-region calls to VPC endpoints or PrivateLink to keep them on the backbone and avoid per-GB NAT charges.
— an EC2 instance calling S3 without a gateway endpoint routes over the public internet through the IGW. Add the endpoint for free and confine S3 traffic to the backbone.
— interface endpoints create private DNS names. Ensure your VPC has `enableDnsHostnames` and `enableDnsSupport` set to true, or DNS resolution falls back to public IPs.

— CloudFront caches content at the edge and excels at HTTP/HTTPS with cacheable responses. Global Accelerator has no cache layer and is better for TCP/UDP applications, gaming, VoIP, and APIs with dynamic responses.
— a single DX connection is a single point of failure. Use two connections into different DX locations, or back up with a Site-to-Site VPN that activates via BGP route preference when DX drops.
— in a TGW hub-and-spoke model, traffic between two spoke VPCs traverses the TGW twice (once in, once out), doubling TGW attachment and data processing charges.
Choosing the Right Backbone Entry Point
Multiple AWS services push traffic onto the backbone; choosing the right entry depends on the workload's protocol, cacheability, direction, and latency requirements.
UDP support, static IP, low jitter
Free, no IGW hop, private routing
Traffic stays in AWS network fabric

AWS owns and operates a private global fiber network connecting all regions, AZs, and edge locations. Inter-region and intra-region AWS service traffic travels on this backbone, not the public internet.
Traffic enters the backbone at the nearest edge PoP (CloudFront or Global Accelerator). Only the first and last mile — the distance between the user and the nearest PoP — uses the public internet.
AWS Global Accelerator exposes the backbone for TCP/UDP workloads, providing anycast entry and backbone routing to your ALB/NLB/EC2 endpoints in any region.
Direct Connect connects your on-premises network directly into the AWS backbone, bypassing the public internet for all enterprise-to-cloud traffic.
Use VPC endpoints (gateway for S3/DynamoDB, interface for others) to keep AWS API calls on the backbone without internet exposure, even from private subnets.
